title: Vanilla Panna Cotta
subtitle: Set cream with vanilla, turned out on a plate or served in the glass
excerpt: Double cream infused with vanilla and set with leaf gelatine, poured into ramekins or glasses and chilled for at least four hours. Turned out, it trembles on the plate. Served in the glass with a spoonful of fresh fruit or a drizzle of sauce on top.
sourceNotes: Panna cotta originated in the Piedmont region of northern Italy, where it was made with cream, sugar, and setting agents. The modern version using gelatine rather than isinglass or other traditional setting agents became standard in the latter half of the twentieth century. It is now one of the most widely served Italian restaurant desserts internationally.

recipe.batchNotes: Make up to three days ahead and refrigerate, covered. Turn out or top just before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Must be made at least 4 hours ahead. Can be made up to three days before serving.

content.text: Panna cotta is a forgiving dessert to make as long as the gelatine quantity is right. Too much and the cream sets hard and rubbery; too little and it won't hold its shape when turned out. The amounts here give a panna cotta that trembles and barely holds together, which is the correct texture. If you plan to serve it in glasses rather than turning it out, you can use slightly less gelatine for an even softer set.

The vanilla pod gives a better flavour than extract here, the seeds are visible in the cream and the flavour is cleaner. Soak the gelatine leaves in cold water until floppy, then squeeze out the excess water thoroughly before dissolving in the warm cream. Gelatine that is too wet will dilute the cream and can prevent a proper set.

items.prepNote: soaked in cold water for 5 minutes

content.text: Six 120 ml ramekins or small glasses, lightly oiled if you plan to turn them out.

Combine {{double-cream}} of double cream, {{whole-milk}} of whole milk, {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, and the vanilla pod and seeds in a medium saucepan. Heat over medium heat, stirring to dissolve the sugar, until just below simmering. Remove from the heat. Lift the soaked gelatine leaves from the cold water, squeeze out the excess water firmly, and add to the warm cream. Stir for 1 minute until completely dissolved. Remove the vanilla pod.
Pour the cream mixture into the prepared ramekins or glasses. Leave to cool to room temperature, then cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours until firmly set.
Turn out or serve in glass

To turn out: dip each ramekin in hot water for 10 seconds, run a thin knife around the edge, and invert onto a serving plate. The panna cotta should drop out. If serving in glasses, top with a spoonful of fresh fruit, a drizzle of raspberry coulis, or a little warm honey.

items.symptom: Panna cotta didn't set and is still liquid
items.cause: Gelatine was not fully dissolved, or not enough gelatine was used
items.fix: Make sure the gelatine is completely dissolved before pouring, stir for at least a minute after adding the leaves. Check the sheet count. If it is still very soft after 4 hours, refrigerate for a further 2 hours.

items.symptom: Panna cotta is rubbery and hard
items.cause: Too much gelatine, or the cream was set somewhere very cold (near the back of the fridge)
items.fix: Use exactly 4 sheets of gelatine for this recipe and keep the fridge at a moderate setting. A rubbery panna cotta cannot be fixed, but the flavour will still be good.

items.symptom: Panna cotta broke apart when turned out
items.cause: Not enough gelatine, or turned out before fully set
items.fix: Make sure the panna cotta has had at least 4 hours in the fridge. Oil the ramekins lightly before pouring, this helps a clean release.

items.symptom: Cream has a slightly grainy texture
items.cause: The cream was boiled rather than just heated, or the gelatine was added to very hot cream
items.fix: The cream should be hot and steaming but not boiling when the gelatine goes in. Boiling cream can cause the protein structure to change.

content.text: Panna cotta is associated with the Piedmont region of northern Italy, where versions of cream set with various setting agents appear in regional cookery from the early twentieth century. The name means simply 'cooked cream'. Its rise as an international restaurant dessert came in the 1980s and 1990s as Italian restaurant cooking became widely popular in Britain and North America. It has the advantage of being both simple to produce in large quantities and very easy to pair with seasonal fruit or sauces.
